subject: ap-chemistry
title: Chemical Equilibrium & Le Chatelier's Principle
stimulus:
5. Hydrogen gas and iodine gas react to form hydrogen iodide at an elevated temperature, as represented by the following equation.
$H_2$(g) + $I_2$(g) ⇌ 2 HI(g)     $\Delta H_{rxn}$ = −12.19 kJ/mol rxn

parts.prompt:
Write the expression for the equilibrium constant, K , for this reaction.
c

scoring_points.description: For the correct equilibrium constant expression: 1 point
wrong_examples: $K_c = \frac{[\text{H}_2][\text{I}_2]}{[\text{HI}]^2}$ — this is the expression for the reverse reaction.
$K_c = \frac{[\text{HI}]}{[\text{H}_2][\text{I}_2]}$ — must include the stoichiometric exponent of 2 on HI.
common_traps: Stoichiometric coefficients become exponents in the equilibrium expression; 2 HI → [HI]².
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct expression: $K_c = \frac{[\text{HI}]^2}{[\text{H}_2][\text{I}_2]}$.

sample_responses.commentary: Products over reactants with stoichiometric exponents: HI has a coefficient of 2, so it appears as [HI]². Earns the point.
This is the expression for the reverse reaction. The equilibrium expression must have products in the numerator and reactants in the denominator. No credit.

parts.prompt:
H (g )and I (g ) are added to a previously evacuated container and allowed to react.
(i)At a certain time, the value of the reaction quotient, Q, is 0.67. The following particle diagram is an
incomplete representation of the system at this time. The diagram shows the relative number of H (g)
and I (g ) molecules, but the HI(g ) molecules are not included. Draw the number of HI(g ) molecules
needed to complete the diagram so that it accurately represents the system.

(ii)A student monitors the number of moles of HI(g ) over time. Hypothesize an experimental change that
could have been applied to the system in the rigid container at time t to result in the change in the
number of moles of HI(g ) shown in the graph. Assume that the student did not add more HI(g ) to the
system.
(iii)After equilibrium is established, the mixture is transferred to a larger container at constant
temperature. As a result, would the number of moles of HI(g ) increase, decrease, or remain the same?
Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (b)(i): Q = [HI]²/([H₂][I₂]) = 0.67; read H₂ and I₂ particle counts from diagram; back-calculate HI count. Part (b)(ii): accept removing H₂ or I₂ (shifting reverse); decreasing temperature for an exothermic reaction shifts reverse; accept adding an inert gas if volume is fixed (no effect) — but the graph shows moles decreasing, so must shift reverse. Part (b)(iii): equal moles — Δn_gas = 0 for this reaction (1+1=2), so changing volume does not shift equilibrium.

scoring_points.description: For drawing the correct number of HI molecules consistent with Q = 0.67: 1 point

required_elements: Using the H₂ and I₂ particle counts from the diagram, solve for n(HI): $Q = \frac{n(\text{HI})^2}{n(\text{H}_2) \cdot n(\text{I}_2)} = 0.67$
Draw the calculated number of HI molecules in the diagram.
alternatives.correct_example: If the diagram shows 4 H₂ and 4 I₂ particles: $0.67 = \frac{n^2}{4 \times 4}$; $n^2 = 10.72$; $n \approx 3$ (round to nearest integer). Draw 3 HI molecules.

wrong_examples: Drawing HI molecules without calculating from Q.
common_traps: In particle diagrams, each particle represents a relative count; the ratio must satisfy the given Q value.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for a correct drawing consistent with part (a): the number of HI molecules drawn must satisfy $Q = [\text{HI}]^2/([\text{H}_2][\text{I}_2]) = 0.67$ using the particle counts shown in the diagram.

sample_responses.response: [If diagram shows 4 H₂ and 4 I₂ particles: draws 3 HI molecules, since $0.67 = n^2/(4 \times 4)$, $n \approx 3$.]
sample_responses.commentary: Uses Q = 0.67 and the given H₂ and I₂ particle counts to back-calculate the HI particle count. The integer rounding is accepted. Earns the point.
Drawing an arbitrary number of HI molecules without verifying it satisfies Q = 0.67 does not earn the point. The number must be consistent with the given Q value.

scoring_points.description: For a valid hypothesis that would decrease moles of HI: 1 point

required_elements: An experimental change that shifts the equilibrium toward reactants (decreasing HI).
Examples: removing H₂ or I₂ (reverse shift); increasing temperature (reaction is exothermic, so heating shifts reverse); removing HI from the container.
alternatives.correct_example: Accept any of the following: (1) Increasing the temperature — since the reaction is exothermic ($\Delta H = -12.19$ kJ/mol), adding heat shifts the equilibrium toward reactants, decreasing [HI]. (2) Removing H₂ or I₂ — shifts the reaction in reverse. (3) Adding a catalyst to accelerate equilibrium in the reverse direction is not a valid answer (catalysts do not shift equilibrium).

wrong_examples: Adding an inert gas (no effect on concentrations in rigid container — does not shift equilibrium).
Adding a catalyst — catalysts do not shift equilibrium.
common_traps: Only changes that affect Q (concentrations, T, or volume) can shift equilibrium; catalysts and inert gases do not.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for a valid hypothesis. Accept: (1) Decreased the temperature. (2) Added more \ce{H2} and/or \ce{I2} to the reaction vessel.

sample_responses.response: The temperature was decreased. Since the reaction is exothermic ($\Delta H = -12.19$ kJ/mol), lowering the temperature shifts the equilibrium toward products, which would increase moles of HI — but the graph shows a decrease. Actually, decreasing temperature for an exothermic reaction shifts toward products (more HI), so this would increase HI, not decrease it. A valid change to decrease HI is: removing some HI from the container, or increasing the temperature.
sample_responses.commentary: CB accepts 'decreased the temperature' — note: for an exothermic reaction, decreasing T favors products (more HI), so the graph context matters. CB's listed accept for a graph showing an increase in HI after t.

sample_responses.response: More \ce{H2} and \ce{I2} were added to the reaction vessel at time t, shifting the equilibrium toward products and increasing the moles of HI.
sample_responses.commentary: Adding reactants stresses the equilibrium toward products, correctly explaining an increase in HI moles. Matches CB's listed accept. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: A catalyst was added at time t, which sped up the reaction and produced more HI.
sample_responses.commentary: Catalysts do not shift the equilibrium position — they only speed up attainment of equilibrium without changing the equilibrium concentrations or moles. No credit.

scoring_points.description: For 'remain the same' with correct reasoning based on Δn_gas = 0: 1 point

required_elements: The reaction H₂(g) + I₂(g) ⇌ 2HI(g) has equal moles of gas on both sides (Δn = 2 − 2 = 0).
Changing volume does not shift this equilibrium; the number of moles of HI remains constant.
alternatives.correct_example: Remain the same. The total moles of gas on each side of the equation are equal: 1 mol H₂ + 1 mol I₂ = 2 mol reactant gas; 2 mol HI product gas. Since $\Delta n_{\text{gas}} = 0$, dilution (increasing volume) has no effect on the equilibrium position, and the moles of HI do not change.

wrong_examples: Saying moles decrease — for reactions with Δn = 0, volume changes do not shift equilibrium.
Invoking Le Châtelier without noting Δn = 0.
common_traps: Le Châtelier pressure/volume changes only affect equilibrium when Δn_gas ≠ 0.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct answer and a valid justification. Accept: (1) Remain unchanged. The number of moles in the numerator and denominator of Q (or K) are equal; changing the volume would not alter Q, which is still equal to K, so the number of moles of HI remains the same. (2) Remain unchanged. The increase in volume decreases concentrations of reactants and products by an equal proportion; because there are equal moles of gaseous reactants and products, there is no shift in equilibrium position.

sample_responses.response: The moles of HI will remain the same. The reaction \ce{H2(g) + I2(g) <=> 2HI(g)} has equal moles of gas on both sides ($\Delta n_{\text{gas}} = 2 - 2 = 0$). Expanding the volume decreases all concentrations equally, so $Q$ remains equal to $K$ and no equilibrium shift occurs.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies Δn_gas = 0, explains why a volume change does not alter Q relative to K, and correctly concludes no shift. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The moles of HI would decrease because increasing volume decreases pressure and shifts equilibrium toward the side with more moles of gas.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrectly applies Le Châtelier's principle for pressure. With equal moles on both sides (Δn = 0), a volume change has no net effect on equilibrium. No credit.
